metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a child (or extend an existing one) that exposes overlap detection as a standalone store-health command (e.g., `rebar validate --overlap` or integration into `next_batch`) independent of the per-ticket review_plan flow.
findings.finding: AC-3 is uncovered: no child delivers the overlap-detection capability as a standalone store-health surface (validate / next_batch integration); the entire detection pipeline is wired exclusively through review_plan by 0f70, which the parent AC-3 explicitly prohibits ('does not run inside the 5fd2 gate').
findings.suggested_fix: Create a sibling child that wires 5a8f+9022 into a `rebar validate --overlap [--ticket <id>]` CLI surface (or extends next_batch's scheduling output), so the capability satisfies the 'store-health / scheduling' placement AC-3 requires, separate from the review_plan path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile 0f70's placement (inside review_plan) with parent AC-3's prohibition ('does not run inside the 5fd2 gate') — either revise AC-3 to permit advisory-only wiring into review_plan, or move the overlap-detection invocation to a separate store-health surface.
findings.finding: 0f70 directly contradicts parent AC-3 by running inside review_plan (the per-ticket plan-review workflow), which AC-3 explicitly states must NOT happen ('does not run inside the 5fd2 gate'); this is a scope-placement conflict between child and parent, not resolvable by reading 0f70 as merely 'advisory'.
findings.suggested_fix: If advisory surfacing during review_plan is the intended design, update parent AC-3 to read 'does not run as a blocking criterion inside the 5fd2 gate' (clarifying blocking vs advisory). If strict separation is required, move all overlap-detection invocation to a standalone CLI/validate surface and remove it from 0f70's review_plan wiring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in 0f70 (or c1de) what signal appears in review_plan output when the digest corpus is empty/sparse (e.g., 'overlap check skipped: corpus not yet enriched — run rebar enrich --drain').
findings.finding: Cold-corpus dependency gap in 0f70: when c1de has not yet drained any entries (corpus of digests is empty or sparse), 0f70 silently returns zero overlap findings with no user-facing signal; neither 0f70 nor c1de specifies the ordering dependency or a corpus-readiness indicator.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an advisory note in 0f70's overlap output section indicating corpus coverage (e.g., 'X of Y tickets have digests; run `rebar enrich --drain` to improve coverage') so users are not misled by silent zero-findings.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether 'store-wide' detection is intended to require proactive full-corpus scanning or whether per-review-time detection is accepted as the delivery model; if the former, add a batch-scan mode.
findings.finding: Scope narrowing on parent AC-1: the detection only fires per-ticket during review_plan invocations, not as a proactive store-wide scan over all ticket pairs — narrowing 'store-wide (any pair)' to 'pairs involving a ticket whose plan is currently being reviewed'.

findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an Acceptance Criterion that makes the 'low false-flagging' goal verifiable — e.g. 'Legitimately-distinct tickets with superficial keyword overlap are NOT surfaced as duplicates (precision validated against a representative sample or explicit threshold).'
findings.finding: The 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work' goal stated in Success Criteria has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion to verify it — it is an uncovered deliverable/goal.
findings.location: Success Criteria (bullet 2) vs Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs)
findings.suggested_fix: Add AC: '[ ] Legitimately related but distinct tickets (e.g. two tickets that touch the same file for different reasons) are not surfaced as duplicates; false-positive rate is bounded by [threshold or sample-based spot-check].'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the suggestion-only (no auto-mutate) design choice — e.g., that semantic overlap detection has inherent false-positive risk and auto-applying links to the live store could silently create incorrect dependency/duplicate relations that block scheduling (next_batch) or mislead agents; confirm this rationale should be preserved even for high-confidence matches.
findings.finding: The plan states 'suggests duplicates/supersedes/depends_on links rather than auto-applying them' as a design choice but provides no positive rationale for WHY suggestion-only is chosen over auto-application. G6 requires that a plan state a positive rationale for its chosen approach; the absence of this rationale leaves an implementer unable to know whether to preserve the suggestion-only posture when edge cases arise (e.g., high-confidence exact-semantic duplicates, or a user flag requesting auto-linking).
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'suggests duplicates/supersedes/depends_on links rather than auto-applying them'; Acceptance Criteria line 2 — 'does not auto-mutate links'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria or a brief 'Why' section, state: 'Auto-application is excluded because semantic similarity scores are inherently probabilistic (false-positive risk) and incorrect duplicates/supersedes links directly affect next_batch scheduling and agent visibility; human confirmation is the circuit-breaker. A future configurable threshold for auto-apply is a follow-on.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a deliverable capturing the architectural decision (ADR or CLAUDE.md section) documenting why this is a store-health capability outside the plan-review gate, and why link mutations are suggested rather than auto-applied.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel store-health pipeline stage with no mention of an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc capturing the architectural decision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default detection approach (e.g., 'use embedding cosine similarity with threshold ≥ 0.85') and define what constitutes 'semantic overlap' so the executor can act without further design decisions.
findings.finding: The detection method for 'semantic overlap' is a real design choice left entirely to the executor — no algorithm, embedding strategy, or similarity threshold is named. An executor cannot implement this without inventing the approach.
findings.location: Scope section; AC line 1 ('Detects semantic overlap/duplication...')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a stated default: e.g., 'Detect overlap via embedding cosine similarity (threshold ≥ 0.85, configurable); keyword/title n-gram overlap as a fallback.' State the threshold explicitly in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace 'low false-flagging' with a measurable, in-session-verifiable criterion (e.g., 'false-positive rate ≤ X% on a reference fixture set of N ticket pairs, verified by a unit test').
findings.finding: 'Low false-flagging' in the Success Criteria is a subjective/unmeasurable term with no precision target, making AC validation impossible in-session.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 2: 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work'
findings.suggested_fix: Define a small fixture set (e.g., 10–20 ticket pairs with known ground-truth overlap/non-overlap labels) and state an acceptance threshold (e.g., precision ≥ 0.80). Gate the success criterion on a deterministic test against this fixture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the output artifact/format for surfaced candidates (e.g., 'written to a JSON report file at `store-health/overlap-report.json`', or 'printed as a structured CLI table') so the executor and human-confirmation workflow are unambiguous.
findings.finding: AC1 states 'detects semantic overlap/duplication' but specifies no observable output format, location, or artifact — an executor cannot know whether detection means a CLI report, a log line, a file written to disk, or a returned data structure.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1/AC2: 'Outputs a structured report (e.g., JSON or CLI table) listing candidate pairs with similarity score, suggested link type, and both ticket IDs, written to a deterministic location for human review.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike/prototype that runs the candidate semantic-similarity approach against a sample of existing store tickets, measures the false-positive and false-negative rates against a small hand-labeled set, and uses the results to derive an initial threshold before committing to full build-out.
findings.finding: The 'low false-flagging' success criterion and the underlying semantic-similarity approach/threshold are asserted without any empirical validation plan. Semantic overlap detection is threshold-sensitive and correctness cannot be established by reasoning alone; no spike, prototype, benchmark, or pilot with a measured false-positive rate is described.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit spike AC: 'Run candidate similarity algorithm (e.g., embedding cosine-similarity with a threshold T) against N representative ticket pairs (hand-labeled: overlap / distinct). Record precision/recall. Adopt T only if false-positive rate < X% on the sample. Document chosen algorithm and threshold derivation in the design.' This de-risks the core heuristic before full implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that overlap/similarity thresholds (and any other tuneable heuristics) are configurable parameters, not hardcoded constants, so false-flagging rate can be adjusted without code changes.
findings.finding: The similarity/overlap thresholds that determine what counts as 'semantic overlap' vs. 'legitimately-related-but-distinct work' are expected to evolve through tuning but the plan does not mention making them configurable rather than hardcoded.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'Overlap sensitivity threshold is exposed as a configurable parameter (e.g., in a config file or CLI flag) defaulting to a documented value, so operators can tune false-positive rate without code changes.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observable acceptance criterion for the false-flagging goal, e.g., a qualitative benchmark scenario (N known-distinct ticket pairs produce zero false flags) or a stated threshold, so reviewers can objectively confirm it is met.
findings.finding: The success criterion 'Low false-flagging of legitimately-related-but-distinct work' is a non-deferred goal with no end-state-observable proof — no metric, acceptance threshold, or test scenario is defined to verify it has been achieved.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete validation mechanism to the Success Criteria or ACs (e.g., a precision/recall target on a labelled sample of known duplicates, a before/after triage-time comparison, or a dogfooding step where the tool is run on the existing store and results reviewed by a named operator).
findings.finding: No concrete validation mechanism is specified for the store-health capability: 'low false-flagging' is qualitative and there is no before/after workflow comparison, precision/recall target, or dogfooding step described in the Success Criteria or ACs.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Success Criteria, add: 'Validated against a hand-labelled sample of ≥20 known duplicate/dependency pairs from the store, achieving ≥80% recall with ≤30% false-positive rate; or demonstrated via a dogfooding run reviewed and signed off by the store maintainer.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a one-sentence positive rationale naming the intended detection approach (e.g., LLM-based pairwise comparison on title+description, or embedding similarity with a cosine threshold, or a validate-time heuristic) and where it runs (a new validate check, a new CLI command, a next_batch signal), so the AC is verifiable and an implementer has a design anchor.
findings.finding: The plan names no mechanism or approach for detecting semantic overlap — not even a choice of direction (embedding-based, LLM pairwise comparison, heuristic keyword clustering, etc.). As a result, an implementer has no implementation target, and the AC ('Detects semantic overlap/duplication between separate tickets store-wide') is unverifiable without a specified detection approach. While this appears intentionally pre-design (the plan is a scope/carve-out ticket), the absence of even a sketch of the intended mechanism means the positive-rationale requirement (G6.4) cannot be satisfied for the core capability.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 1 — 'Detects semantic overlap/duplication between separate tickets store-wide (cross-epic AND non-epic), beyond deterministic file-impact and manual links.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before committing to a custom build, research and document: (a) how GitHub Issues / Jira / Linear surface duplicate/similar tickets; (b) which detection technique (embedding cosine-similarity, BM25, LLM pairwise, heuristic) fits rebar's store-scale and optionality model; and (c) whether an existing OSS library (sentence-transformers, rank_bm25, or the existing [agents] embedding stack) covers the need — then state and justify the chosen approach in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan names no detection approach (embedding similarity, BM25, LLM pairwise comparison, etc.) and does not consider established external prior art — GitHub Issues, Jira, and Linear all ship similar/duplicate-issue detection; sentence-transformers, OpenAI embeddings, and BM25 libraries are established solutions for exactly this problem. A semantic NLP capability being built custom with no stated approach and no comparison to existing tools violates T1(a) and T1(b).
findings.location: Success Criteria / Acceptance Criteria (whole plan — no Implementation section exists)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
